content:
Java wants compact data without making developers abandon ordinary classes

7 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Jonathan Castañeda on Unsplash1. Background
If you have ever replaced a small Java class with a few primitive arrays for performance, you already understand the pain behind Project Valhalla.
The code becomes faster, yes. It also becomes less readable, easier to misuse, and harder to refactor. We trade names, constructors, validation, and methods for raw layout. So Crazy! But many performance-sensitive Java systems have done some version of this for years.
The recent attention around JEP 401 — Value Classes and Objects comes from a concrete milestone. Around June 2026, the Java community started discussing the arrival path for Valhalla work in the JDK 28 timeframe. The widely circulated integration number is large — more than 197,000 lines across 1,816 files. The official JEP page, however, still describes the feature with Java NN placeholders and lists it as a preview language and VM feature, so we should be careful: this is not a finished production guarantee.
JEP 401 describes value objects as a “preview language and VM feature.”
That sentence matters. Valhalla is not just syntax. It touches the language, class…
